question_text: A 6-year-old boy presents with fever. On examination, he shows small white vesicles around his mouth and on his gum. Small discrete cervical lymph nodes are also palpable. Which of the following viruses is more likely to be associated with these symptoms?

A 4-year-old girl is brought to the clinic for prolonged diarrhoea. On examination, she looks pale. She is drinking 3 pints of goat''s milk/day, and a very fussy eater (see lab results). Test Result Normal Values Hb 59 112-165 g/L MCV 102 80-95 fL MCHC 390 260-340 g/L Which of the following is the most significant in the past medical history?

A 10-month-old presents with fever, vomiting and copious watery diarrhea for the past 3 days. examination reveals a lethargic child with cool, dry skin, sunken eyes, tachycardia and mild tachypnea. Which of the following tests is the most likely confirm the diagnosis?

A 6-year-old child is brought to the Paediatric Clinic. The parent reports intermittent fever, vomiting, diarrhea, and diffuse abdominal pain for the past week. The parent indicated that the child''s eyes also appears to have a yellowish skin stain for the past 2 days. The liver is palpable 5 cm below costal margin and there is jaundice, scleral icterus, and mild abdominal tenderness on examination. Blood pressure 110/70 mmHg Heart rate 76 /min Respiratory rate 18 /min Temperature 38.0° C Which of the following is the most likely type of viral hepatitis?

A 15-month-old child is brought to the clinic with one day of fever and vomiting. A lumbar puncture is performed (see lab results and report). CSF exam Test Result Normal Values Total protein 300 150-450 mg/L Glucose 3 2.50-4.8 mmol/L Cells 1200 0-3 /µL CSF Culture: Gram positive cocci in chains, pus cells and WBCs with a predominance of Neutrophils. Which of the following is the most appropriate antibiotic or antibiotic combinations?

A 9-year-old boy who presents to the Emergency Department with fever, headache, sore throat, and vomiting. He recently arrived from Africa with his parents. On examination, he shows abnormal reflexes, stiff neck and difficulty in lifting his head and legs when he was lying flat on his back. His laboratory investigations show a clear CSF with lymphocytosis, normal glucose level, and normal protein level. No bacteria were seen. Which of the following is the most likely organism responsible for his illness?

A 3-day-old baby is brought to the clinic with fever, vomiting and hypothermia. Prior to labour his mother was febrile. He was examined and lumbar puncture was performed (see report). Cerebrospinal Fluid Gram stain: Showed Gram positive bacilli and on culture it was Beta hemolytic and catalase positive. Which of the following antibiotics should be started empirically?
Group B streptococci are a common cause of neonatal meningitis. Vaginal swab for group B streptococci is recommended in pregnant mother. At which gestational age should the vaginal swab be collected?

A 7-year-old child is brought to the clinic with fever, headache and vomiting of 2 days. On examination, there is meningeal irritation. A lumbar puncture is done (see lab results and report). CSF Analysis Test Result Normal Values Total Protein 560 150-450 mg/L Glucose 2.9 2.50-4.8 mmol/L Bacteriology Report: Gram positive cocci in pairs. Which of the following antibiotic combination would be most appropriate?

A full-term infant developed bilateral conjunctivitis at 2 weeks of age. The conjunctivitis is followed by severe coughing but the infant remained febrile. When the infant was 4 weeks old, a chest X-ray showed bilateral symmetrical interstitial infiltrates (see lab results). Test Result Normal Values WBC 14 4.5-10.5 x 109 /L Neutrophils 32 40-60% Lymphocytes 15 20-40 % Monocytes 2 2-8 % Eosinophils 4 1-4 % IgG 30 7.5-19 g/L IgA 8 1-4 g/L IgM 3.5 0.52-1.52 g/L Which of the following is the most probable infectious agent?

A 3-year-old girl presents with a high-grade fever and chills. Physical examination, confirms that she is anemic with splenomegaly. Clinical Image with giemsa stain is shown (see image). Which of the following is the most likely etiology?

A full term boy was born to a mother diagnosed antenatally with hepatitis B surface Ag positive (HBAg positive). Her delivery was unremarkable and the newborn weighed 3.4 kg and was normal on assessment. He received the hepatitis B vaccine and hepatitis B Immunoglobulins within the first 4 hours. The mother is very enthusiastic to breastfeed her child as she did with her previous children. Which of the following is the best advice?

A 4-year-old child suddenly developed a high fever, chills, and vomiting. Examination shows nuchal rigidity and a positive Kemig''s sign. Petechial lesions are noted on both extremities and over the abdomen. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 2-year-old, severely ill child has been irritable and pyrexial for 2 days. The child started vomiting, appeared drowsy, and has deteriorated rapidly during the day. In addition, a petechial rash started today which has spread extremely quickly around the body. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 1-month-old infant referred to the dental clinic with presentation of white plaques visible on the surfaces of the dorsal tongue and hard palate, which can be wiped off. A clinical diagnosis was made and the infant was started on nystatin 4 times a day for a period of 10 days. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 15-year-old child presents to the Outpatient Clinic suffering from abdominal pain, diarrhea, and tenesmus for the last 2 weeks. There is mucous and blood present in the stool. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 4-year-old has a 3-day history of fever, irritability, and anorexia. Examination confirms painful vesicles over the mucosa of the soft palate and posterior pharynx, sparing the gingiva and anterior oral cavity. Blood pressure 110/70 mmHg Heart rate 120 /min Respiratory rate 30 /min Temperature 39.0° C Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 12-year-old child was prescribed antibiotics for treatment of a chest infection. 6 weeks after cessation of antibiotics, the patient presents with fever, abdominal pain, and profuse watery stools for the past month. Investigation confirms fecal leukocytes. Which of the following is the most likely pathogen?

A 7-year-old child is brought to the Emergency Department with a history of fever and vomiting for 2 days. On examination, he is dehydrated, comatose and acidotic (see lab results). CSF Test Result Normal Values Cells 20 0-3 / uL Total Protein 0.45 0.22-0.33 g/L Glucose 3.5 2.50-4.8 mmol/L Which of the following is the most likely the diagnosis?

An 18-month-old child presents to the Emergency Department after a brief, generalized tonic clonic seizure. He has had a large, watery stool that had both blood and mucus in it. He is now postictal. Blood pressure 100/60 mmHg Heart rate 110 /min Respiratory rate 28 /min Temperature 40.0° C Which of the following is the most likely cause?

A 1-year-old girl presents to the Paediatrics Emergency Department at night with episodes of loud cough and refusal to feed. There is a history of upper airway infection with nasal congestion and sneezing over the last week. She has not received any routine immunizations. During the physical examination, there are repetitive violent fits of a hacking cough that are followed by forceful, high-pitched inhalations and accompanied by facial cyanosis. Between attacks the girl appeared exhausted and apathetic. Heart rate 150 /min Respiratory rate 60 /min Temperature 36.6° C Oxygen saturation 92% on room air Which of the following is the most likely cause?

A 1-year-old girl presents to the Pediatrics Emergency Department at night with episodes of loud coughing and refusal to feed. There is a history of upper airway infection with nasal congestion and sneezing over the last week. She has not received any routine immunizations. During the physical examination, there are repetitive violent fits of a hacking cough that are followed by forceful, high-pitched inhalations accompanied by facial cyanosis. Between attacks the girl appears exhausted and apathetic. Heart rate 150 /min Respiratory rate 60 /min Temperature 36.6° C Oxygen saturation 92% on room air Which of the following diagnostic test would be most appropriate?

A 5-year-old child is brought to the clinic with a febrile illness with a cough and separate irregularly shaped (blotchy) rashes. He has not had any routine vaccinations. On physical examination, there is enlarged tender cervical lymph nodes. There are also erythematous maculopapular rashes behind the ear, face and neck and to a lesser extent on the trunk. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 6-year-old child presents with a low-grade fever, malaise, conjunctivitis, coryza, and a cough. Recently, a rash of discrete pink macules began on the face and extends to the hands and feet. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
A 6-month-old infant develops a cough, coryza, fever, expiratory wheezing, grunting, tachypnea, rib retractions, and air trapping. Which of the following is optimal immediate treatment?

A 6-year-old child presents with malaise, fever, cough, nasal discharge, and conjunctivitis. Several days later the patient develops a maculopapular rash starting on the face and rapidly spreading to the rest of the body. Which of the following viruses is most likely the cause?

A 3-year-old child presents with a 1-week history of malaise, anorexia, nasal discharge, and conjunctivitis. The parent states that the child has also had 2 days of paroxysmal coughing spells with a stridor 'whoop.'" Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?"

A 3-year-old boy presents to the Paediatric Emergency Department with fever, headache and generalized muscular pain over the last 24 hours. The child appears sleepy and irritable. There are several spots on the buttocks and legs. Physical examination confirms stiffness of the neck. After performing a lumbar puncture, an intravenous infusion of penicillin G is started (see image, lab result and report). Blood pressure 90/55 mmHg Heart rate 120 /min Respiratory rate 38 /min Temperature 39° C Oxygen saturation 95% on room air Test Result Normal Value WBC 25 4.5-10.5 x 109/L CSF Culture: Gram Negative diplococci. Which of the following is the most likely late complication?

A 3-year-old boy presents to the Pediatric Emergency Department with fever, headache and generalized muscular pain over the last 24 hours. The child appears sleepy and irritable. There are several spots on the buttocks and legs. Physical examination confirms stiffness of the neck. After performing a lumbar puncture, an intravenous infusion of penicillin G is started. The parents also report that the child''s 6-year-old brother had been in close contact with this boy and ask how best to look after him (see image and report). Blood pressure 90/55 mmHg Heart rate 120 /min Respiratory rate 38 /min Temperature 39° C Oxygen saturation 95% on room air CSF analysis: Elevated cell count and Gram Negative diplococci. What would be the best management for the older brother?

A 5-year-old boy presents with a sore throat and refusal to eat. The mother reported that the child has had an increased temperature for the last 3 days. Several children that attended the same play group have developed similar symptoms. Physical examination shows spots and blisters on the palm of both hands as well as on the buttocks. There are small ulcers on the palate (see image). Heart rate 110 /min Respiratory rate 30 /min Temperature 38.6° C Which of the following additional physical findings is most likely to have?

A 10-year-old boy presents to the Paediatric Clinic with a skin rash. The parents report that the symptoms had started with a single red spot over a small scratch and have spread over the 4 days. There are several lesions on the cheeks, the trunk and the upper limbs. Physical examination confirms swollen lymph nodes in the arm pits and groins (see image). Blood pressure 100/65 mmHg Heart rate 80 /min Respiratory rate 24 /min Temperature 37.4° C Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment?

A 10-year-old boy presents to the Paediatric Clinic with a skin rash. The parents report that the symptoms had started with a single red spot over a small scratch and have spread over the 4 days. There are several lesions on the cheeks, the trunk and the upper limbs. Physical examination confirms swollen lymph nodes in the arm pits and groins (see image). Blood pressure 100/65 mmHg Heart rate 80 /min Respiratory rate 24 /min Temperature 37.4° C Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 6-year-old girl presents with ear pain and swelling of the pinna. She had returned the previous day from swimming camp and developed these complaints 1 day before. On examination, she has significant pain when moving the pinna and a foul smelling discharge from the ear canal. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?

An 18-month-old child is brought to the Emergency Department with fever, irritability and refusal to move the left lower extremity. Physical examination confirms a swollen, erythematous knee with resistance to passive mobilization. Which of the following would be the single most important test?

A 3-year-old child presents to the Emergency Department with a history of bloody diarrhea and oliguria. The parents report that the child had started a fever, vomiting, diarrhea and abdominal pain 7 days before, shortly after they ate at a fast-food restaurant. On physical examination, the child appears ill and in distress. She is also pale and lethargic (see lab results). Test Results Normal Values Hb 83 112-165 g/L WBC 22 4.5-10.5 x 109/L Platelets count 50 150-400 x 109 /L Urinalysis Results Normal Values Proteins + Absent Erythrocytes 10 0-2 per high power field Which of the following is the most appropriate management strategy?

A 6-year-old child presents with a high-grade fever, malaise, conjunctivitis, coryza, and a cough. A rash of discrete pink macules recently began on the face and extends to the hands and feet. The child also had small, whitish spots inside the mouth before the rash appeared. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
A 4-month-old boy presents to the clinic with rash over the neck for 3 days. The rash is not itchy (see image). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
Which of the following is the most frequent agent causing acute bronchiolitis?

An 8-year-old sickle cell child arrives at the Emergency Department in respiratory distress. Over the previous 3 days, the child has become progressively tired and pale (see lab result). Test Result Normal Value Hb 31 112-165 g/L Which of the following is the most likely cause?

A 3-year-old alert and oriented girl presents to the Paediatric Emergency Department at 2 a.m. with breathing difficulties and a brassy, barking cough. There is inspiratory stridor at rest and a hoarse voice. The girl has been suffering from a viral upper airway infection for the past week. A physical examination shows moderate chest wall retractions. On auscultation, there is decreased air entry. The extremities and buccal membranes appear pink and moist. Heart rate 180 /min Respiratory rate 60 /min Temperature 39.9 C rectal Oxygen saturation 93% on room air Which of the following is the most appropriate management?

A 7-month-old presents with low-grade fever and a dry cough for the last 3 days. On examination, there is wheezing over both lung fields (see report). Heart Rate 127 /min Respiratory Rate 47 /min Temperature 38.9° C Chest X-ray: Hyperinflation and some infiltrate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 2-year-old is brought to the clinic with sore throat and high-grade fever for 5 days. On examination, he is febrile and mildly dehydrated. The pharynx and tonsillar area is congested along with inflammation of the mouth and lips associated with gingivitis. Lesions in the mouth and lips appear as whitish-yellow plaques with erythematous base. Which of the following is the most likely cause?

An 8-year-old girl presents with a sore throat and a temperature of 38°C. History reveals, she has not received any childhood immunisation. A clinical examination confirms a severely swollen and edematous neck region with enlarged cervical lymph nodes. Her pharynx is erythematous with bleeding spots when a swab is taken for culture. An inspiratory stridor is noted upon auscultation of the lungs. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 1-year-old girl is brought to the Paediatric Emergency Department in the early morning with breathing difficulties. She has a dry cough that sounds like the barking of a sea lion and gets worse when she is crying. Every time the infant breathes in, there is a high-pitched sound. There is also a 4-day history of a mild upper airway infection. Blood pressure 80/65 mmHg Heart rate 120 /min Respiratory rate 40 /min Temperature 38.5° C Which of the following is the most appropriate way to establish the diagnosis?

A 6-month-old boy presents to the paediatrician with a loud barking cough and a rectal temperature of 38° C. The parents report a cold for the last 3 days that worsens at night. The coughing is aggravated with crying and there is a high-pitched, rasping sound on inhalation. Physical examination confirms a reddened throat and a nasal obstruction (see report). Chest X-ray: Normal. Which of the following is the most likely cause?

A 3-year-old girl presents to the Paediatric Emergency Department at night with a barking, dry cough and laboured breathing. There is a 3-day history of nasal obstruction and cough that had worsened over the last 2 hours. The child appears anxious and cries with a hoarse voice. There is a sharp, high-pitched sound on inspiration. Physical examination confirms indrawing of the chest wall and a reddened throat. A single treatment with nebulized epinephrine results in a quick improvement of the airway symptoms. 30 minutes after the inhalation the coughing and the breathing difficulties reappear. Which of the following is the next step in management?

An 8-month-old child presents to the Pediatric Emergency Department in the early morning hours. He has laboured breathing and a barking cough that becomes worse when crying. There is a 2-day history of nasal congestion and dry cough. Physical examination confirms nasal flaring and a reddened throat. There is also a harsh, high-pitched sound when the child breathes in. Auscultation over the lung fields is normal. The boy is anxious and cries with a hoarse voice. Heart rate 110 /min Respiratory rate 30 /min Temperature 39° C Oxygen saturation 94% on room air Which of the following is the most likely cause?

A 12-month-old boy presents to the Pediatric Emergency Department in the early morning hours with nasal congestion and a barking cough and 3 days history of a mild airway infection. Physical examination shows suprasternal retractions and nasal flaring. The back of the throat is reddened. Lung fields are clear on auscultation. The inhalation of nebulized epinephrine temporarily improves the symptoms. Heart rate 110 /min Respiratory rate 30 /min Temperature 38.5° C Oxygen saturation 94% on room air Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 4-year-old boy presents to the Paediatric Emergency Department at 3 a.m. with a barking cough and mild respiratory distress. The parents report that he had an airway infection over the last 2 days that had suddenly worsened. The boy appears irritable. Physical examination confirms an inspiratory stridor. The child speaks with a hoarse voice. Blood pressure 90/65 mmHg Heart rate 110 /min Respiratory rate 30 /min Temperature 38° C Oxygen saturation 92% on room air Which additional finding would be cause for concern?

A 3-year-old girl presents to the Paediatric Emergency Department in the early morning hours with a barking cough, that worsens with crying. There is a history of mild cough and temperatures around 38° C (rectal) over the last 3 days. Physical examination confirms an inflamed throat and mild suprasternal retractions. Blood pressure 95/65 mmHg Heart rate 90 /min Respiratory rate 34 /min Temperature 39.3° C Which of the following is the most likely at auscultatory finding?

A 13-year-old girl presented to the clinic with fever and painful cervical lymph nodes. On examination, tonsils were covered by white exudate. Temperature 39 °C Which of the following is the most likely complication to develop?

A 4-year-old child presented with anorexia, low-grade fever, watery bloodless diarrhea, vomiting, abdominal cramps and dehydration 2 days after exposure to a case of diarrhea in day care center. Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment?

A 2-month-old infant present to Emergency Room with cough, sneezing, circumoral cyanosis occasional with frothy secretion from mouth for the 3 days. On examination, she looks well with moderate tachypnea and intercostal retraction. Chest reveals scattered rhonchi and crackles all over but good air entry. Blood pressure 80/50 mmHg Heart rate 138 /min Respiratory rate 48 /min Temperature 36.6 °C Oxygen saturation 91 % Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 6-month-old infant with cough and fever diagnosed as bronchiolitis. On examination, she had mild distress with intercostal retraction and chest auscultation reveals diffuse crackles. Blood pressure 92/67 mmHg Heart rate 145 /min Respiratory rate 56 /min Temperature 36.6 °C Oxygen saturation 89 % Which of the following is the best management?
option1: Admit for oxygen and hydration therapy
option2: Send home with instruction when to come back
option3: Admit for antibiotics and oxygen administration
option4: Admit for bronchodilation and steroid management
correct_option: Admit for oxygen and hydration therapy

question_text: A 2.5-year-old boy seen in the clinic with skin rash. There is history of mild abdominal pain but the child looks healthy otherwise (see image). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 6-year-old child seen in the clinic with fever and sore throat. She has had contact with child developed flu-like symptoms. Throat exam showed mild hyperaemia. Rapid throat test was negative for streptococcal infection and throat swab sent for culture Blood pressure 110/70 mmHg Heart rate 76 /min Respiratory rate 18 /min Temperature 36.6 °C Oxygen saturation 95 % Which of the following is the best management at this stage?
option4: Start amoxicillin for 10 days regardless of culture result

question_text: A 9-month-old infant presents with history of fever, irritability and right ear rubbing. Ear exam showed air-fluid levels and bulging tympanic membrane. Blood pressure 70/50 mmHg Heart rate 110 /min Respiratory rate 18 /min Temperature 38.8 °C Oxygen saturation 95 % Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
Which of the following organism is the most causative agent in paediatrics acute otitis media?
A 3-year-old child present to the clinic with skin lesion that is not itchy. No history of fever and no previous illness (see image). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 4-year-old boy previously healthy came to the clinic with history of limping for the last 2 days. On physical examination, he is keeping his left leg flexed and refusing to move it or allowing anyone to touch it. Imaging is showing effusion of the left hip. Blood pressure 90/61 mmHg Heart rate 126 /min Respiratory rate 29 /min Temperature 38.8 °C Oxygen saturation 98 % Which of the following is the most likely causative organism?

A 3-month-old girl presented to the Emergency Room with symptoms of paroxysmal episodes of cough followed by whooping sound at the end. It is occasionally associated with perioral cyanosis and post- tussive vomiting. These symptoms have gotten worse when started to have fever of 39.5° C. History revealed she did not receive her 2-month vaccine. Which of the following is the most common expected complication?

A 3-month-old girl presented to the Emergency Room with symptoms of paroxysmal episodes of cough followed by whooping sound at the end. It is occasionally associated with perioral cyanosis and post- tussive vomiting. History revealed that she did not receive her 2-month vaccine. Her mother has already given her 2 doses of antimicrobial treatment. What is the gold standard method to diagnose this condition?

A 3-month-old boy presented to the Emergency Room with history paroxysmal episodes of cough followed by whooping sounds at the end of each episode. It is associated with perioral cyanosis and post-tussive vomiting. There is no history of diarrhea. This illness has been preceded by runny nose a few days ago. History revealed he did not receive his 2-month vaccine yet. On examination, the child looks unwell and tachypneic. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 3-month-old boy presented to the Emergency Room with history paroxysmal episodes of staccato cough, inspiration between each cough. It is associated with perioral cyanosis and post-tussive vomiting. There is no history of diarrhea. This illness has been preceded by conjunctivitis few days ago. On examination, the child looks unwell and tachypneic with auscultatory crackles. He has normal white blood cell count with elevated eosinophils on laboratory evaluation. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 3-month-old boy presented to the Emergency Room with history paroxysmal episodes of cough associated with an inspiratory whoop and possessive emesis. It is associated with fever, conjunctival redness and diarrhea. His immunization is up to date. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 9-month-old boy presents to the clinic with skin rash noticed by his parent over the past few weeks. His parents are known to have eczema before. Which of the following is the typical site of eczematous plaques at this age?

An 8-year-old boy presents to the clinic with skin rash noticed by his parent over the past few weeks. His parents are known to have eczema before. Which of the following is the typical site of eczematous plaques at this age?

A 14-year-old boy presents to the clinic with skin rash noticed by his parent over the past few weeks. His parents are known to have eczema before. Which of the following is the typical site of eczematous plaques at this age?

A 6-year-old boy who has been treated since infancy for eczema is coming now to be evaluated for honey-colored crusted lesion in antecubital skin area bilaterally. He has no fever but skin infection is suspected. Which of the following is the most likely cause of the skin infection?

A 6-year-old boy who has been treated since infancy for eczema is coming now to be evaluated for severely itching umbilicated vesico pustular skin rash that is grouped in 'clusters of grapes” appearance overlying diffuse eczematous plaques. A superimposed skin infection is suspected. Which of the following is the most likely etiology?"

A 5-year-old boy who has been treated for eczema with increasing strength of topical steroid is presenting with worsening eczematous plaques on flexural surfaces of his elbows and knees. Which of the following is the best next step in the management?
option3: Referral to dermatologist to start narrow-band UV-B phototherapy
option4: Inquire about parent compliance with the treatment and answer their concerns
correct_option: Inquire about parent compliance with the treatment and answer their concerns

question_text: A 10-year-old girl presented to the clinic with pruritic, eczematous plaques over her flexural region and xerosis. Good bathing practice and adequate hydration are recommended to the patient. Which of the following is considered a good bathing practice recommendation?
option1: Emollients are not important after good prolonged bathing
option2: Olive oil is superior to coconut oil in decreasing severity
option3: Using strong soap cleansers is more effective than non-soap cleansers
option4: Emollient choice is highly dependent on provider and patient predilection
correct_option: Emollient choice is highly dependent on provider and patient predilection

question_text: A 5-year-old boy presents to the clinic with pruritic, eczematous plaques over his flexural region and xerosis. Good bathing practice and adequate hydration are recommended to the patient. Which of the following is considered a good bathing practice recommendation?
option1: Using non-soap cleansers is preferred to avoid irritation
option2: Emollients are not important after good prolonged bathing
option3: Lotions have been proven to be more effective than creams
option4: Olive oil is superior to coconut oil in decreasing severity
correct_option: Using non-soap cleansers is preferred to avoid irritation

question_text: A 3-month-old girl is hospitalized for confirmed pertussis infection. She is started on macrolide therapy. She has 2 other siblings at home, 3 and 5 years old who are both up to date for their vaccinations. Which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation for her asymptomatic sibling?
option3: Close observation for signs and symptoms of pertussis
option4: Booster vaccine with DTaP if they are from high risk group

question_text: A 4-month-old girl with unilateral purulent effusion, decreased tympanic membrane mobility with pneumatic otoscopy, no fever, and no other symptoms. She is breastfeeding very well and does not use pacifier. She has no recent history of infections. Which of the following is the best next step in management?
option4: ENT referral for evaluation of tympanostomy tubes

question_text: A 19-month-old boy presented to the clinic with right lower eyelid swelling, erythema, and tenderness for 1 day. He has fever reaching up to 38.5° C with decreased appetite and activity. He was given diphenhydramine without any improvement of the eyelid swelling. His parent denied any visual disturbances, diplopia or any other swelling. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A previously healthy 13-month-old girl has 2-day history of fever and increasing cough. She continued to be breastfed with no vomiting. Her immunizations are up to date. She is alert and mildly ill appearing. On examination, there is no grunting or chest retractions. There are crackles heard over the right lung base. The remainder of the examination findings are normal. She has no known allergies. Blood pressure 110/70 mmHg Heart rate 146 /min Respiratory rate 45 /min Temperature 38.6 °C Oxygen saturation 95 % Which of the following is the most likely pathogen?

A 7-year-old girl is complaining of sore throat for the last 2 days. Today, she has developed fever of 38.7 °C when measured at home, which has been relieved by acetaminophen. On examination, she was found to have tender anterior cervical adenopathy and pharyngeal and tonsillar exudate. The rest of her physical exam is unremarkable. Which of the following is best next step toward diagnosis?
option4: Fine needle aspiration for cervical adenopathy

question_text: A 4-year-old girl presented to the clinic with history of fever for last 3 days associated with sore throat and decrease oral intake. She also has difficulty in swallowing solid food. On examination, she has palpable cervical lymph node that is about 2 cm in size. Which of the following is the most important step in evaluation?
option4: Fine needle aspiration of the lymph node

question_text: A 4-year-old girl presented to the clinic with history of fever for last 3 days associated with sore throat and decrease oral intake. She also has difficulty in swallowing solid food. Her vaccination is up to date. On examination, she has palpable cervical lymph node that is about 2 cm in size, tender and fluctuates. Which of the following is the most likely causative organism?

A 5-year-old boy is diagnosed with group A streptococcus tonsillitis and started on antibiotic treatment. He has a 2-year-old brother who is well and asymptomatic. Which of the following is the best approach for his brother?
option4: Throat swab for rapid antigen detection test

question_text: A newborn baby boy is born to unbooked 25-year-old mother. The mother has developed febrile illness immediately after birth but she is otherwise stable. She does not have record for any of the transmissible infections. She asked if she can breastfeed her baby. In which of the following conditions breastfeeding or expressed breast milk in the immediate postpartum period is acceptable?
option2: Expressed breast milk of HIV mother
option4: Expressed breast milk of untreated active tuberculosis
correct_option: Expressed breast milk of untreated active tuberculosis

question_text: A 2-week-old neonate is brought by his mother with complains of poor oral intake for the last 2 days. On examination, he has oral thrush and diaper dermatitis. Which of the following is the most applicable management?
option4: Oral and topical anti-fungal for 5 days

question_text: A 3-year-old child presented with fever, headache, difficulty swallowing and throat pain. On examination, there is intense inflammation of the tonsils with purulent exudate that is more membranous in appearance. Blood pressure 110/70 mmHg Heart rate 150 /min Respiratory rate 28 /min Temperature 38.8 °C Oxygen saturation 96 % Which of the following is the most likely causative organism?

A 7-month-old baby presented to the clinic with history of fever, irritability and disturb sleeping for 2 days. Ear examination showed perforation of the eardrum with pus visible in the external canal. Blood pressure 110/70 mmHg Heart rate 115 /min Respiratory rate 22 /min Temperature 38.8 °C Oxygen saturation 97 % Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 7-month-old baby presented to the clinic with history of fever, irritability and disturb sleeping for 2 days. Ear examination showed perforation of the eardrum with pus visible in the external canal. Blood pressure 110/70 mmHg Heart rate 115 /min Respiratory rate 24 /min Temperature 39 °C Oxygen saturation 97 % Which of the following is the most common causative organism?

A 3-year-old child presented to Emergency Room with complains of fever, headache and lethargy. On examination, clear purpuric rash, sign of shock and positive Kernig sign present. Eye exam revealed the presence of papilledema. The parents are bedside and worried about child condition and prognosis. Which of the following complication is important for the parents?
Which of the following represent a fetal complication from maternal infection with Parvovirus B19?
option2: May lead to fetal hydrops and death
option4: Severe fetal skin disease with eye involvement
correct_option: May lead to fetal hydrops and death
